senses.definition: Using or involving photoelectric effects that are produced or controlled by a device itself, without outside action.
examples: The engineer tested an autophotoelectric sensor in the laboratory.
Autophotoelectric cells can adjust their own sensitivity to light.
The device relies on an autophotoelectric mechanism to trigger the alarm.

senses.definition: A photograph that a surface or object produces of itself under light, without a traditional camera.
examples: The scientist examined the autophotograph formed on the treated plate.
Under strong sunlight, an autophotograph appeared on the material.
They archived the autophotograph as evidence of the reaction.

senses.definition: The automatic measurement of light or brightness, usually by a device measuring its own illumination.
examples: Autophotometry is used to control the brightness of the display.
The instrument relies on autophotometry to adjust exposure.
Modern cameras include autophotometry for better image quality.

senses.definition: An ophthalmoscope that allows a person to examine their own eye.
examples: The patient used an autophthalmoscope to monitor changes in the retina.
An autophthalmoscope can help users detect eye problems early.
The clinic demonstrated how to handle the autophthalmoscope safely.

senses.definition: The production or development of leaves by a plant from its own tissues, without help from other organisms.
examples: Researchers studied autophyllogeny in the rare plant species.
Autophyllogeny can be observed as new leaves form after damage.
The paper describes the mechanisms of autophyllogeny in ferns.

senses.definition: A plant that makes its own food from simple substances, usually through photosynthesis.
examples: Algae are often classified as autophytes in ecological studies.
An autophyte can survive without taking nutrients from other organisms.
The pond is rich in microscopic autophytes.

senses.definition: Plants that make their own food from simple substances, usually through photosynthesis.
examples: In this ecosystem, autophytes form the base of the food chain.
The lake contains many tiny autophytes.
Scientists measured the biomass of autophytes in the wetland.

senses.definition: Able to make food from simple substances, without taking nutrients from other organisms.
examples: The algae show autophytic growth in the pond.
Autophytic organisms can live in nutrient-poor environments.
The researchers focused on autophytic species in the sample.

senses.definition: In a way that involves making food from simple substances without taking nutrients from other organisms.
examples: The bacteria live autophytically in the deep sea.
Some organisms can switch between living autophytically and parasitically.
The species grows autophytically under strong light.

senses.definition: An image or record produced directly by a plant or its movements, usually on a prepared surface.
examples: The vine left an autophytograph on the coated plate.
They studied the autophytograph to track the plant’s growth.
The experiment produced a clear autophytograph of the leaf pattern.

senses.definition: The process or technique by which a plant produces an image or record of itself, usually on a prepared surface.
examples: Autophytography was used to document the plant’s daily movements.
The paper explores autophytography as a tool for plant research.
Through autophytography, the roots created delicate patterns.

senses.definition: A device that automatically controls the direction or movement of an aircraft, ship, or other vehicle without constant control by a person.
examples: The airplane was flying on autopilot for most of the journey.
The captain switched off the autopilot before landing.
Modern ships rely heavily on autopilot systems.

senses.definition: A mental state in which you do things automatically, without really thinking about them.
examples: I drove home on autopilot and barely remembered the trip.
He goes through his morning routine on autopilot.
When you are tired, you tend to act on autopilot.

senses.definition: Devices that automatically control the direction or movement of aircraft, ships, or other vehicles without constant control by a person.
examples: Modern autopilots can handle complex flight paths.
The company designs autopilots for small drones.
Autopilots are standard in commercial jets.

senses.definition: In Spanish-speaking countries, a wide, fast road for cars, usually with several lanes and no crossings, similar to a motorway or freeway.
examples: We drove along the autopista to reach the city faster.
There was a serious accident on the autopista.
You have to pay a toll to use this autopista.

senses.definition: In Spanish-speaking countries, wide, fast roads for cars, usually with several lanes and no crossings, similar to motorways or freeways.
examples: This region is connected by several modern autopistas.
Autopistas reduce travel time between big cities.
They plan to build new autopistas along the coast.

senses.definition: The act of reusing your own written work, data, or ideas in a new piece, especially in academic or professional settings, without saying that you used them before.
examples: The journal warned authors about autoplagiarism in submissions.
Resubmitting the same essay to another course is considered autoplagiarism.
Policies on autoplagiarism differ between universities.

senses.definition: A medical treatment that uses a patient's own blood plasma or similar material to treat their disease or injury.
examples: Autoplasmotherapy was suggested as a possible treatment option.
The clinic specializes in autoplasmotherapy for joint problems.
Researchers are testing new forms of autoplasmotherapy.

senses.definition: A piece of living tissue taken from a person's own body and used in a surgical repair on that same person.
examples: The surgeon used an autoplast to reconstruct the damaged area.
Skin taken as an autoplast reduced the risk of rejection.
An autoplast is often preferred over donor tissue.

senses.definition: Relating to the use of a person's own tissue in surgical repair.
examples: The team chose an autoplastic procedure for the patient.
Autoplastic surgery often leads to better long-term results.
The autoplastic method lowered the risk of immune reactions.

senses.definition: In psychology, describing changes a person makes in themselves to cope with stress or problems, rather than trying to change the outside world.
examples: Her response to conflict was mainly autoplastic.
Autoplastic adaptation focuses on changing one's own behavior.
The therapist described his reaction as autoplastic, not alloplastic.

senses.definition: In a way that uses a person's own tissue in surgical repair.
examples: The wound was autoplastically reconstructed.
The damaged bone was autoplastically replaced.
The surgeon operated autoplastically to reduce rejection.

senses.definition: In a way that involves changing oneself to deal with stress or problems.
examples: He reacted autoplastically by changing his own behavior.
The patient adapted autoplastically to the new environment.
They coped autoplastically rather than trying to change others.

senses.definition: Surgical operations where tissue from a person's own body is used to repair or replace damaged parts.
examples: Autoplasties are common in reconstructive surgery.
The clinic reported successful autoplasties on burn patients.
Several autoplasties were performed during the mission.

senses.definition: Surgery in which tissue from a person's own body is used to repair or replace damaged parts.
examples: Autoplasty was chosen to reconstruct the damaged ear.
The hospital is known for its advances in autoplasty.
Autoplasty can reduce the risk of tissue rejection.

senses.definition: Using air pressure that is produced or controlled by the device or system itself.
examples: The machine includes an autopneumatic control unit.
Autopneumatic systems reduce the need for external compressors.
They designed an autopneumatic valve for the prototype.

senses.definition: A brand name for a mechanical pencil with a push or twist mechanism to advance the lead.
examples: He always wrote with an Autopoint because he liked mechanical pencils.
The old Autopoint still works after many years.
She bought an Autopoint for her drawing class.

senses.definition: Mechanical pencils of the Autopoint brand.
examples: The store sells several models of Autopoints.
He collects vintage Autopoints.
Autopoints were popular among architects.

senses.definition: Poisoning or harming itself by substances it produces.
examples: Under stress, the organism becomes autopoisonous.
An autopoisonous reaction can kill the cells.
The plant secretes autopoisonous chemicals in closed systems.

senses.definition: Having an electrical or magnetic polarity that arises within the system itself, without outside influence.
examples: The crystal shows an autopolar structure.
Autopolar materials can generate internal electric fields.
The study focused on autopolar properties of the compound.

senses.definition: A sport or exhibition in which people ride in cars instead of on horses while playing a game like polo.
examples: Autopolo became popular in the early twentieth century.
The museum showed old photos of autopolo matches.
Autopolo required strong cars and brave drivers.

senses.definition: A person who plays autopolo.
examples: The autopoloist drove aggressively towards the ball.
Every autopoloist wore protective gear.
Famous autopoloists drew large crowds.

senses.definition: An organism, especially a plant, that has more than two sets of chromosomes, all from the same species.
examples: The crop is an autopolyploid with four chromosome sets.
Autopolyploids often show larger cell size.
They compared diploids and autopolyploids in the study.

senses.definition: Having more than two sets of chromosomes, all from the same species.
examples: The researchers created autopolyploid strains in the lab.
Autopolyploid plants can be more vigorous than diploids.
They studied autopolyploid lines of wheat.

senses.definition: Conditions in which organisms have more than two sets of chromosomes, all from the same species.
examples: Autopolyploidies are common in certain plant groups.
The paper reviews known autopolyploidies in crops.
Different autopolyploidies can affect fertility.

senses.definition: Organisms, especially plants, that have more than two sets of chromosomes, all from the same species.
examples: Many autopolyploids show increased size.
The field contains both diploids and autopolyploids.
Autopolyploids can arise spontaneously in nature.

senses.definition: The state of having more than two sets of chromosomes, all from the same species, or the process that leads to this state.
examples: Autopolyploidy plays a role in plant evolution.
The researchers induced autopolyploidy in the laboratory.
Autopolyploidy can change flower size and shape.

senses.definition: A pore or tiny opening that forms by itself in a material or biological structure.
examples: The membrane developed an autopore under high pressure.
Water passed through a single autopore in the surface.
Autopores can affect the strength of the material.

senses.definition: A picture, especially a painting or drawing, that an artist makes of themselves; a self-portrait.
examples: The museum displayed the painter’s only autoportrait.
She painted an autoportrait when she was twenty.
His latest work is an abstract autoportrait.

senses.definition: The art or practice of creating pictures of oneself; self-portrait art.
examples: Her work focuses on autoportraiture and identity.
Autoportraiture became common in modern photography.
The course explores the history of autoportraiture.

senses.definition: Developing directly as a positive image, instead of first making a negative.
examples: They used an autopositive process to produce the print.
Autopositive film was preferred for quick results.
The camera supports an autopositive mode.

senses.definition: Living or growing in still fresh water that is not strongly influenced by external flow.
examples: The species is autopotamic, found mainly in quiet ponds.
Autopotamic plants prefer calm, fresh water.
Researchers listed several autopotamic organisms in the area.

senses.definition: Having the power to act, develop, or control something by itself.
examples: The system is autopotent and does not need external input.
Autopotent cells can organize their own development.
He described the process as autopotent and self-regulating.

senses.definition: Advancing or developing by its own internal processes, without outside help.
examples: The disease is autoprogressive and worsens over time.
They observed autoprogressive changes in the material.
The model assumes an autoprogressive pattern of growth.

senses.definition: The process in which an enzyme or protein breaks down its own structure.
examples: Autoproteolysis reduced the activity of the enzyme.
They monitored autoproteolysis during the experiment.
Excess heat can trigger autoproteolysis of the protein.

senses.definition: A surgical replacement or support made from a person's own tissue.
examples: The surgeon used an autoprothesis for the joint repair.
An autoprothesis can reduce rejection compared with artificial implants.
They developed a new type of autoprothesis for heart valves.

senses.definition: The act of seeing something with your own eyes; direct personal observation.
examples: He reported the event based on autopsia, not on rumors.
Autopsia is important for accurate historical accounts.
Her conclusion came from autopsia of the site.

senses.definition: Acts of seeing something with your own eyes; direct personal observations.
examples: The historian’s claims rest on several autopsias.
Autopsias of the battlefield influenced his writing.
They valued autopsias more than written sources.

senses.definition: Based on seeing something with one’s own eyes or on direct personal observation.
examples: The historian offered an autopsic description of the ruins.
Autopsic evidence supported his argument.
Her report was autopsic rather than second-hand.

senses.definition: Relating to direct personal observation of something with one's own eyes.
examples: The book contains autopsical accounts of the disaster.
He preferred autopsical data over reports from others.
Autopsical detail made the narrative vivid.

senses.definition: Examined a dead body by cutting it open to find the cause of death; performed an autopsy on.
examples: The coroner autopsied the body the next morning.
Several victims were autopsied after the accident.
The animal was autopsied to determine the disease.

senses.definition: Medical examinations of dead bodies by cutting them open in order to find the cause of death.
examples: The report included results from three autopsies.
Autopsies showed that the patients died from heart failure.
In many cases, autopsies are not requested by families.

senses.definition: A person, usually a doctor, who performs autopsies.
examples: The autopsist prepared a detailed report for the court.
As an experienced autopsist, she had seen many unusual cases.
The hospital hired a new autopsist for the pathology department.

senses.definition: People, usually doctors, who perform autopsies.
examples: Autopsists testified about their findings at the trial.
The conference gathered autopsists from several countries.
Few autopsists are trained in rare diseases.
